fixtures.name: paradigm_break_requests_session_mount
fixtures.rationale: httpx uses a mounts= dict passed to Client() constructor — there is no public .mount() method and no HTTPAdapter subclassing; requests.auth.AuthBase.__call__(), init_poolmanager() override, create_urllib3_context(), and session.mount() are requests/urllib3-specific APIs with no httpx equivalent

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_urllib3_pool
fixtures.rationale: httpx abstracts connection pool management entirely; urllib3.HTTPSConnectionPool, pool.request(), and explicit pool.close() have no httpx equivalent — connection lifecycle is internal to Client/transport

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_aiohttp_session
fixtures.rationale: httpx uses transport= not connector=, and response objects are returned directly rather than used as async context managers; aiohttp.ClientSession(connector=...) and `async with session.get() as resp:` are not httpx patterns

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_sync_in_async
fixtures.rationale: importing requests and calling requests.get() inside async def blocks the event loop; httpx provides httpx.AsyncClient with await client.get() as the correct async transport

fixtures.name: paradigm_break_raw_socket
fixtures.rationale: http.client.HTTPSConnection with conn.request() / conn.getresponse() / conn.close() is the low-level stdlib API that httpx abstracts; callers never construct connection objects or manage their lifecycle in httpx

fixtures.name: control_client_context_manager
fixtures.rationale: idiomatic httpx sync usage: httpx.Client() with transport=, event_hooks=, timeout=, auth= keyword arguments as context managers; response.status_code assertions and raise_for_status() patterns identical to httpx test corpus

fixtures.name: control_async_client_transport
fixtures.rationale: idiomatic httpx async usage: httpx.AsyncClient() with transport=, auth=, timeout= as async context manager; await client.get/post with status_code assertions; named handler functions (not lambda) for MockTransport
